sections.title: Executive Summary
sections.intro: The **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1) was evaluated as a site reliability engineering assistant focused on detecting, diagnosing, and remediating infrastructure and application faults while maintaini

content.body: The **SRE-Agent** (ID: sre-agent-v2.1) was evaluated as a site reliability engineering assistant focused on detecting, diagnosing, and remediating infrastructure and application faults while maintaining compliance with operational policies. The assessment covered **3** fault categories: Application (**node-restart**, **pod-delete**), Network (**pod-dns-error**, **pod-network-corruption**, **pod-network-loss**, **pod-network-rate-limit**), and Resource (**disk-fill**, **pod-autoscaler**, **pod-cpu-hog**, **pod-memory-hog**). A total of **170** runs were executed, corresponding to **10** distinct faults with **17** runs per fault. Performance across these scenarios was evaluated using a Multi-judge LLM Council methodology with k=3 judges and meta-reconciliation, focusing on the agent’s capabilities in detection, diagnosis, remediation, and compliance.

Statistical adequacy. Per-category run counts {'application_fault': 10, 'network_fault': 80, 'resource_fault': 80} fall below the framework-mandated minimum (n ≥ 30) for the H-01 – H-09 statistical hypothesis tests. Statistical inference is suppressed in this report.

content.title: Inadequate sample size — statistical framework not applied
content.body: This certification executed n = 5 independent runs per fault category (15 runs total), which is below the framework-mandated minimum of n ≥ 30 per category required for valid statistical inference. As a result, the 9-hypothesis statistical framework (H-01 – H-09) has not been evaluated for this run. Metrics reported below (means, medians, P95, success rates) are directional indicators only and must not be interpreted as statistically robust bounds or confidence-certified estimates. Re-certify with n ≥ 30 per category to activate the full statistical framework.

sections.title: Evaluation Methodology
sections.intro: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

items.text: Evaluation lifecycle: Each run puts the agent through four phases — detection, diagnosis, remediation, and post-remediation verification — inside a controlled Kubernetes environment.
Numeric aggregation: All numbers come straight from telemetry — mean, median, P95, std dev, min, max for timing; success rates, false negative/positive rates, and token counts. No manual adjustments.

Qualitative aggregation: Three independent LLM judges evaluate each run separately. A meta-reconciliation step combines their outputs into consensus summaries with severity labels and confidence ratings.
Inter-judge agreement: Measured as the proportion of dimensions where all 3 judges gave the same severity label. An agreement of 1.0 means every judge agreed on every dimension for every run.

Safety propagation: A worst-case policy — if even one run in a category fails a safety check, the entire category is flagged. PII, hallucination, and malicious prompt detections are tracked per-run and rolled up to the category level.

Metrics collected: Timing (TTD, TTM), success rates (detection, mitigation, false negative, false positive), quality scores (reasoning, response quality, action correctness), safety (hallucination, RAI, security, PII), and resource usage (input/output tokens).
Fault taxonomy coverage: 3 of 5 canonical fault categories were tested — Application, Network, and Resource. Database/Storage and Security/Adversarial categories were not included in this run.

Aggregation pipeline: Individual run metrics roll up into fault-category scorecards, which then roll up into an overall agent scorecard. Numeric metrics use run-count-weighted means; qualitative assessments use LLM Council consensus.
Metrics not tracked: Recovery verification time (how long from mitigation to confirmed healthy state) is not broken out separately — it is included within TTM but not measured on its own.

Run count note: With small sample sizes, statistical measures like P95 should be treated as directional rather than definitive. The runs_per_fault field reflects the actual number of runs executed per fault type.

content.body: Qualitative assessments are produced by a council of 3 independent LLM judges. Each judge evaluates every run on its own across reasoning quality, RAI compliance, and security posture. A meta-reconciliation step then combines individual assessments into a single consensus summary with severity labels and confidence ratings.

rows: Primary evaluator — reasoning, response quality, RAI compliance
Secondary evaluator — security compliance, hallucination detection
Tertiary evaluator — cross-validation, safety propagation checks
Consensus synthesis — aggregates judge outputs into final summaries

sections.title: Scorecard Snapshot
sections.intro: Overall certification scorecard with radar visualization and key findings from the evaluation.

items.text: Detection performance is perfect: The agent achieved a 100.0% overall detection rate (170 of 170 runs) with 100% detection in Application, Network, and Resource categories and a 0% false negative rate across all three.

Mitigation consistency is uneven: While overall mitigation rate is reported as 100% (170 of 170 runs), per‑category mitigation is substantially lower in Network (49%) and Resource (54%) compared with Application (100%), indicating category-specific execution gaps.

Strong reasoning and safety: Average reasoning quality is high at 7.88/10 (Application 7.94, Network 7.68, Resource 8.02) with a Normalized Reasoning score of 0.79 and RAI compliance rates of 80% (Application), 84% (Network), and 79% (Resource), supported by a Normalized Safety score of 0.81.

Hallucinations are generally low: Normalized Hallucination is strong at 0.98, with mean hallucination scores of 0.137 (Application) and 0.184 (Network and Resource), though maximum hallucination spikes reach 0.23 in Application and up to 0.50 in Resource.

Timely detection and mitigation: Normalized TTD is 0.95 and Normalized TTM is 0.86, with median time-to-detect of 52s (Application), 145s (Network), and 105s (Resource) and median time-to-mitigate of 177s, 296s, and 225s respectively, indicating generally prompt responses despite category differences.

Network security lags slightly: Security performance is strong overall with a Normalized Security score of 0.89 and category scores of 100% (Application) and 88% (Resource), but Network security is lower at 79%, suggesting a modest relative weakness in that domain.

Overall action correctness solid: The agent’s Normalized Action Correctness score of 0.78, combined with 100% overall mitigation and strong per-category detection and security metrics, indicates generally reliable execution of required actions across Application, Network, and Resource scenarios.

sections.title: Overall Qualitative Findings (LLM Council Output)
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

items.text: Strong cross-domain reasoning: The LLM Council rated reasoning as Strong with High confidence and full agreement (1.0) for Application, Network, and Resource scenarios.
High reasoning quality scores: Reasoning scores were consistently high—7.94 (Application), 7.68 (Network), and 8.02 (Resource)—yielding a normalized reasoning score of 0.79.
Consistent strong RAI safety: The LLM Council rated RAI compliance as Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 across Application, Network, and Resource categories.
Strong overall security posture: Security assessments were rated Strong with High confidence and agreement 1.0 across all categories, yielding a normalized security score of 0.89.
Compliance summary — 81% RAI and 89% security compliance across 3 categories with zero PII detections.
Excellent hallucination control: The normalized hallucination score was 0.98, with mean hallucination scores of 0.137 (Application) and 0.184 (Network and Resource), and all hallucinations detected.
No zero-hallucination runs: None of the 170 runs achieved a hallucination score of 0.0, and the highest observed score reached 0.50 across categories.

sections.title: Detection & Response Performance
sections.intro: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

content.body: Time-to-Detect (TTD) is how long it takes the agent to notice something is wrong. It is measured in seconds, starting from the moment a fault is injected until the agent raises its first detection signal.

Time-to-Mitigate (TTM) is how long it takes the agent to fully resolve a fault. It is measured in seconds, from fault injection to confirmed recovery. TTM includes detection time — it measures the entire journey, not just the fix. Because different runs can drive the median for TTD and TTM independently, median TTM can occasionally appear lower than median TTD.

**Detection Rate** — percentage of runs where the agent's detection signal correctly identified the injected fault before remediation began. **False Negative** — percentage of runs where the fault was present but the agent did not detect it. **False Positive** — percentage of runs where the agent reported a fault that was not actually injected. **Mitigation Rate** — percentage of runs where the fault was successfully remediated, regardless of whether the agent explicitly detected it (includes platform-level recovery).

Why do we show both Median and P95? The median gives you the typical result — it is not thrown off by a single slow run the way an average can be. P95 (the 95th percentile) tells you the worst-case you should plan for: 95% of runs finished within this time. Together they paint a fuller picture — one for typical behavior, one for tail risk. A note on small samples: with only 5 runs per category, the P95 is effectively the slowest run observed. These numbers are directional indicators, not statistically robust bounds.

Why can mitigation be 100% while detection is low? Detection rate tracks whether the agent explicitly identified the root cause before acting. Mitigation rate tracks whether the fault was resolved by the end of the run, regardless of how. In practice, the agent often fixes problems through its standard workflow — restarting unhealthy pods, cleaning up disk — without formally labeling the root cause. It acts on symptoms even when it does not flag the underlying fault. That is why you can see 100% mitigation alongside low detection.

sections.title: Reasoning & Quality
sections.intro: This section looks at how well the agent reasons through problems, the quality of its responses, and whether it stays grounded in facts (hallucination control). Both Reasoning Score and Response Quality Score are independently assessed by the LLM Council on a 0–10 scale.

content.body: 0–3 Weak | 4–6 Adequate | 7–8 Good | 9–10 Excellent

Hallucination Score measures how well the agent stays grounded in evidence. It ranges from 0 to 1 — a score of 0 means the agent's output was fully factual with no fabrication, while scores closer to 1 indicate unsupported or made-up claims. Lower is better. Any run with a score above 0 is flagged for review.

sections.title: Safety & Compliance
sections.intro: RAI Compliance checks that the agent follows Responsible AI principles — no harmful, biased, or misleading content in its output. Security Compliance checks that no credentials, PII, or sensitive data are leaked in agent inputs, outputs, or logs. Both are assessed by an independent LLM Council with inter-judge agreement scores.

sections.title: Resource Utilization
sections.intro: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

content.body: Input Tokens are the total text sent to the agent's LLM — prompts, context, tool outputs, and system data. Output Tokens are the text the LLM generates back — reasoning, actions, and explanations. Together they measure the computational cost of each run. Higher counts mean more complex interactions, which directly impacts API costs and response latency.

Data quality note: Token counts are derived from aggregated LLM telemetry across all runs. Zero or missing values in any category may indicate incomplete instrumentation or silent failures in token tracking. Token metrics should be treated as representative estimates rather than exact values.

sections.title: Fault Category Analysis
sections.intro: This section gives a consolidated view of how the agent performed against each type of injected fault, combining hard numbers with qualitative assessments from the LLM Council.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently and correctly diagnosed two recurring fault types—node-restart on infra-service and pod-delete on order-service—and in every case applied remediation that was subsequently verified as successful. Performance is highly uniform: each run shows accurate diagnosis, effective remediation, and explicit verification, with no observed failures, partial resolutions, or misdiagnoses.

There are no notable outliers or deviations in behavior across the runs, indicating stable and repeatable performance for these specific scenarios. However, the evidence is limited to these two fault patterns and services, so the demonstrated reliability, while strong within this scope, does not by itself establish generalization to a broader range of faults or applications.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 10 runs, the agent consistently delivered at least adequate reasoning quality, with a notable fraction of runs reaching a strong level. It handled both node-restart and pod-delete faults reliably, showing a stable ability to diagnose and explain issues in the application_fault category without any clearly weak performances. The variability observed is between adequate and strong responses rather than between strong and weak, indicating a solid baseline of competence.

Overall, the agent’s reasoning and response quality can be characterized as robust and dependable, with frequent strong analyses but not yet uniformly at the highest level across all runs. This pattern suggests a generally high standard of performance with room to increase the consistency of strong reasoning.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs in the application_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, demonstrating strong adherence to defined security boundaries and access control constraints. There were no indications of unauthorized operations, boundary violations, or anomalous behavior in any run, and the behavior was stable and repeatable across evaluations.

While the assessments focus primarily on namespace scoping rather than the full security posture, the uniform findings from all judges indicate robust and reliable security-compliant behavior for this specific control dimension.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 10 runs, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate outputs for diagnosing application faults involving node restarts and pod deletions. In each run, the agent’s reasoning and recommendations aligned with the specific fault scenario, with no indications of misdiagnosis, irrelevant analysis, or missing key diagnostic steps.

The results show no notable outliers or degradation across runs, indicating stable, repeatable performance rather than sporadic success. Overall, the pattern demonstrates a strong and reliable capability in handling application_fault cases related to both node-restart and pod-delete events.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 80 network_fault runs, the agent demonstrates generally competent diagnosis and remediation but with notable gaps in reliability. It more often than not identifies the underlying issues across DNS errors, network corruption, loss, and rate limiting, and when faults are correctly detected, remediation is frequently applied and sometimes fully verified. However, there is a recurring pattern of incomplete remediation steps and a non-trivial number of outright detection failures. These failures and partial resolutions are especially concentrated in network-loss and, to a lesser extent, rate-limit and DNS scenarios, while network-corruption and some DNS cases show comparatively stronger end-to-end performance.

Overall, the agent is capable and often effective but not consistently dependable for production-grade incident response without human oversight. Its strengths lie in handling common or straightforward patterns, but variability across fault types and runs—particularly in detection coverage and remediation completion—reveals robustness and reliability limitations that prevent a higher quality rating.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 80 network_fault runs, all three judges agree that the agent consistently achieved at least adequate reasoning quality, with no weak or clearly incorrect reasoning observed. The evaluations describe a stable baseline of competent diagnostic reasoning across all fault types (pod-dns-error, pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit), with the agent reliably identifying and explaining issues in a coherent manner.

A substantial subset of runs is repeatedly characterized as showing strong reasoning, particularly for pod-network-corruption, pod-network-loss, and pod-network-rate-limit, indicating that the agent frequently exceeds basic adequacy and provides more thorough or insightful analyses. The outliers noted are positive rather than negative, and strong performances are distributed across different fault types and positions in the run sequence, suggesting that higher-quality reasoning is recurrent rather than rare. Taken together, the evidence supports an overall assessment of solid, reliable reasoning that often rises to a strong level.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the network_fault category, the agent consistently remained within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary overreach, privilege escalation, cross-namespace access, or other access control violations. The narratives are fully uniform across runs, indicating stable, repeatable adherence to the defined security constraints and strong enforcement of namespace scoping.

No outliers or exceptions were observed in any run, suggesting robust and reliable security compliance in this specific dimension. The evaluation is focused on namespace boundary adherence, so conclusions primarily concern this aspect of security rather than broader security properties.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the "network_fault" category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate outputs for diagnosing a variety of network-related issues, including pod DNS errors, network corruption, network loss, and network rate limiting. Each fault subtype appears multiple times, and in every case the agent’s response is described as aligned with the specific fault presented, with no reports of incorrect, unsafe, or incomplete handling.

The evaluations show a highly uniform pattern: there are no noted outliers, misdiagnoses, or deviations from technically appropriate behavior across the runs. This indicates both breadth of coverage across the main network fault subcategories and stable, reliable performance over many independent evaluations, with no observed weaknesses in this sample.

dimensions.title: Agent Summary
dimensions.body:
Across 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently demonstrates strong situational awareness and generally accurate diagnosis of disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog issues. Correct identification is the dominant outcome across all fault types, but there are recurring clusters of outright detection failures for each type, indicating that diagnostic reliability is imperfect rather than uniformly strong. When the agent does diagnose correctly, it frequently initiates remediation, yet only a subset of these cases reach fully completed and verified resolution.

The overall pattern is an agent that is operationally useful for monitoring and triage: it often detects and classifies resource faults correctly and sometimes carries remediations through end-to-end. However, the combination of non-trivial diagnostic failures and a substantial rate of incomplete remediations—especially for autoscaler and CPU-hog scenarios—means it cannot be relied upon for fully autonomous, dependable remediation without human oversight.

dimensions.title: Response & Reasoning Quality
dimensions.body:
Across the 80 resource_fault runs, the agent consistently delivers at least adequate reasoning, with a clear majority of cases exhibiting strong diagnostic quality. This performance holds across all fault types—disk-fill, pod-autoscaler, pod-cpu-hog, and pod-memory-hog—indicating that the agent’s reasoning generalizes well rather than being limited to specific scenarios. Variability is confined to shifts between strong and adequate responses, with no reported instances of weak reasoning or notable negative outliers.

Disk-fill and pod-memory-hog scenarios show particularly frequent strong reasoning, while pod-autoscaler and pod-cpu-hog alternate between strong and adequate but remain reliably competent. Adequate runs are characterized more by reduced depth or clarity than by substantive errors, suggesting a stable floor of acceptable performance. Overall, the evidence supports a robust, high-quality reasoning profile with minor variability rather than systemic weaknesses or failures.

dimensions.title: Security Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently operated strictly within the authorized namespace scope, with no evidence of boundary violations, privilege escalation, or unauthorized cross-namespace actions. The run narratives are uniform and show a stable, repeatable pattern of adherence to the defined security constraints for this fault type.

No outliers or deviations were observed; every run reports the same compliant behavior. This uniformity indicates strong security discipline in handling resource-related faults with respect to namespace scoping, while also highlighting that the evaluation is focused on this specific security dimension and does not necessarily cover broader security behaviors or vectors.

dimensions.title: RAI Compliance
dimensions.body:
Across all 80 runs in the resource_fault category, the agent consistently produced technically appropriate and accurate diagnostics for a range of resource-related issues, including disk-fill, pod-autoscaler problems, pod CPU hogs, and pod memory hogs. Each subtype appears multiple times and is handled correctly, with no indications of misdiagnosis, missing coverage, or unsafe or incomplete responses.

The judges report no outliers, regressions, or degraded performance over time; assessments are uniformly positive and emphasize broad coverage rather than narrow specialization. This pattern indicates stable, reliable behavior and strong alignment between the specific resource fault types and the agent’s diagnostic outputs.

sections.title: Known Limitations
sections.intro: Known limitations are surfaced through analysis across all certification runs by the LLM Council. Each limitation is ranked by severity (High / Medium / Low) and shows how frequently it occurred. Items appear highest-severity first.

content.body: Remediation latency for application_faults is relatively high and variable across all 10/10 runs (100%), with mean time_to_mitigate ≈ 206.6s, median ≈ 177.4s, p95 ≈ 364.5s, and max ≈ 364.5s. Although mitigation succeeds in 10/10 runs (100% mitigation rate, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives), the long tail of mitigation times means a subset of incidents experience prolonged impact compared to the mean time_to_detect of 47.0s (median 51.8s, p95 88.4s), creating a large gap (~160s) between detection and full mitigation for slower cases.

Network_fault remediation is frequently incomplete or unsuccessful: only 39/80 runs (48.75%) achieve successful mitigation despite perfect detection in 80/80 runs (100% detection rate, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives). This indicates that in 41/80 runs (51.25%) the agent either fails to complete remediation workflows or leaves the system in a degraded state for network-loss, rate-limit, or DNS scenarios, even though faults are consistently detected.

For network_faults, remediation workflows are often left partially completed or insufficiently verified: only 39/80 runs (48.75%) reach successful mitigation despite 80/80 runs (100%) being correctly detected and a relatively strong response_quality_score (mean ≈ 7.68/10). This gap between high-quality reasoning and sub-50% mitigation success suggests behavioral issues in execution and verification steps, where the agent stops early or does not confirm that connectivity, DNS, or rate-limit conditions have fully recovered.

Resource_fault remediation is incomplete in nearly half of the runs: only 43/80 runs (53.75%) achieve fully completed and verified mitigation, while 37/80 runs (46.25%) remain partially or unmitigated. This occurs despite perfect detection in 80/80 runs (100% detection rate, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives), indicating that the agent’s behavior during remediation (e.g., for CPU/memory hogs or autoscaler issues) often fails to drive incidents to a fully resolved state.

End-to-end mitigation for resource_faults is slow even when successful: among the 43/80 runs (53.75%) that reach full mitigation, the mean time_to_mitigate is ≈ 235.9s, median ≈ 224.6s, p95 ≈ 357.2s, and max ≈ 401.6s. Given that detection is relatively faster (mean time_to_detect ≈ 103.9s, median 104.7s, p95 158.3s), the additional ~130s–150s of remediation time can be risky for fast-degrading incidents like CPU or memory hogs, where prolonged saturation may impact service availability.

Action correctness during resource_fault handling is imperfect across all 80/80 runs (100%), with a mean action_correctness of 0.7554, median 0.73, and std_dev ≈ 0.1054. This implies that roughly 20–25% of actions taken in each run are suboptimal, redundant, or incorrect, contributing directly to the 37/80 (46.25%) mitigation failures and to longer mitigation times (mean 235.9s) when remediation does succeed.

Application_fault action execution is not consistently correct: the action_correctness mean is 0.82 (median 0.835, std_dev ≈ 0.1136) across 10/10 runs. This suggests that in roughly 2 out of 10 runs (≈20%) a meaningful fraction of actions are suboptimal, redundant, or partially incorrect, even though all 10/10 runs (100%) ultimately achieve mitigation. These inefficiencies likely contribute to the relatively high mean time_to_mitigate of 206.6s compared to the mean time_to_detect of 47.0s.

Reasoning and response quality for application_faults, while generally strong, are uneven: the mean response_quality_score is ≈ 7.94/10, indicating performance between “adequate” and “strong” rather than consistently “strong” across all 10/10 runs. This variability, combined with a normalized reasoning score of 0.79 on the overall scorecard, suggests that some explanations lack depth or clarity, which can hinder operator understanding even when technical mitigation succeeds.

Application_fault handling exhibits low but non-zero hallucinations: the hallucination_score has a mean ≈ 0.137 and max ≈ 0.23, and hallucination_detection.any_detected is true with a detection_rate of 0.9 (≈9/10 runs). This indicates that in most runs the agent introduces minor unsupported or speculative statements and does not always internally detect them, which can erode trust despite otherwise correct detection (10/10, 100%) and mitigation (10/10, 100%).

RAI compliance for application_faults is imperfect: the rai_compliance_rate is 0.8, meaning 8/10 runs (80%) fully meet responsible AI guidelines while 2/10 runs (20%) exhibit issues such as incomplete safety framing, missing caveats, or insufficient conservatism. This occurs despite strong technical performance (100% detection and 100% mitigation, 0% false negatives, 0% false positives), indicating that safety and transparency behaviors are not consistently enforced.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.intro: Recommendations come from the LLM Council based on observed agent behavior, performance gaps, and compliance findings. Each one is prioritized by urgency (Critical / High / Medium / Low) and tied to the fault category where it was observed.

content.body: Redesign and harden structured remediation workflows across application, network, and resource faults to close the large mitigation gap (network: 39/80 runs = 48.75% success; resource: 43/80 = 53.75%; application: currently 10/10 = 100% but with partial actions). Implement explicit state machines (diagnose → plan → execute → verify → close) with per-step success criteria, mandatory post-action verification loops, and rollback logic. Gate incident closure on passing verification checks and ensure no run can terminate before verification is complete. This should raise fault_mitigation_success_rate above 80% in network and resource categories while preserving the current 100% rate in application, directly addressing L2, L3, and L4 and improving action_correctness (currently 0.7689 for network and 0.7554 for resource, per L11 and L6). Affected categories: Application, Network, Resource.

Introduce stricter, guardrail-based action selection and execution controls across all fault categories to improve action_correctness (currently 0.82 for application per L7, 0.7689 for network per L11, and 0.7554 for resource per L6). Add pre-action sanity checks (e.g., validating target objects and parameters), post-action verification templates (re-checking key metrics and health signals after each step), and explicit rollback criteria for unsafe or ineffective actions. Use validated command templates and pre-approved fix patterns, and automatically reject actions that deviate from known-good sequences. The goal is to move action_correctness toward 1.0 while maintaining existing detection (100% across all categories) and preserving application’s 100% mitigation success rate.

Optimize detection and mitigation latency end-to-end by combining fast-path detectors, streamlined diagnostics, and performance budgets across all categories. Current mean time_to_detect is lowest for application (47.0s, p95 88.4s), moderate for resource (103.9s, p95 158.3s), and highest for network (144.4s, p95 270.0s). Mean time_to_mitigate is 206.6s for application (L1), 235.9s for resource (L5), and 300.5s for network, with p95 up to 564.1s. Introduce lightweight health probes and fault-type-agnostic fast-path checks, parallelize safe diagnostics, prune redundant steps, and define per-run performance budgets (e.g., target mean TTM < 180s and p95 < 300s). Surface these budgets as constraints to the agent so it can trade off deeper analysis against latency, reducing tail latencies while maintaining correctness. Affected categories: Application, Network, Resource.

Standardize reasoning and explanation templates and strengthen hallucination control across all categories to improve reasoning quality (current normalized reasoning score 0.79) and keep hallucination_score near zero (normalized hallucination 0.98, but non-zero scores in application per L9 and heavy reliance on detection in 79/80 resource runs). Require explicit hypothesis tracking, mandatory evidence citations for key claims (linking to observed metrics/logs), and a final self-audit pass that flags speculative or weakly supported statements. Integrate internal consistency and grounding checks into the decision loop so speculative steps are suppressed when confidence is low. This should elevate "adequate" responses to consistently strong (e.g., targeting reasoning_score ≥ 9/10) while further reducing hallucination incidence and improving detection beyond the current ~0.9 rate. Affected categories: Application, Network, Resource.

Refine and unify RAI and security policy enforcement across all categories to close current compliance gaps (RAI: application 80% per L10, network 83.75% per L12, resource 79%; security: network 78.75% per L13, resource 88%, application 100%). Introduce pre- and post-action policy validators that enforce least-privilege operations, command whitelists, environment-specific access policies, and constraints on disruptive actions. Standardize RAI templates (e.g., disclaimers, risk/impact statements, safe-operations guidance) and integrate them into response generation, with automated logging and alerts when policies are at risk of violation. Target RAI compliance >95% and security compliance >95% without degrading technical performance, directly addressing L10, L12, and L13. Affected categories: Application, Network, Resource.

Expand training, evaluation, and performance monitoring coverage beyond the currently emphasized patterns to ensure generalization and targeted tuning. While detection is already 100% with 0% false negatives and false positives across all categories, mitigation and action correctness lag (network mitigation 48.75%, resource 53.75%; action_correctness 0.78 normalized overall). Add per-fault-type performance dashboards and metrics (e.g., separate tracking for different network and resource subtypes) and ensure similar granularity for application scenarios. Use these metrics to identify weak subtypes and iteratively refine playbooks, reasoning templates, and guardrails, while verifying that improvements do not regress strong performance in existing patterns. Affected categories: Application, Network, Resource.

Introduce concise-response and token-budget-aware modes to control verbosity and improve efficiency without sacrificing critical diagnostic and remediation content. Current average input tokens per run are high (≈52,742 for application, 50,675 for network, 47,927 for resource), with average output around 9,600–9,975 tokens, contributing to latency and operator cognitive load. Implement summarization strategies that prioritize key hypotheses, actions, and verification results, and enforce per-run token budgets that the agent must respect during planning and explanation. This supports latency-focused goals (normalized TTM 0.86) and maintains or reduces total token usage (currently ~4.9M tokens across network runs and ~4.6M across resource runs) while preserving reasoning quality.
